summary: O'Neil critiques Kinvolved, a new app that tracks Harlem schoolchildren's attendance and sends alerts to parents. While the app may have good intentions and could help some families, she argues the data it generates about poor Black and brown children is inadequately protected. She highlights that the user agreement can be changed at any time and that bankruptcy could lead to data being sold to brokers. FERPA's loopholes allow such apps to access education records without meaningful constraints on future use.
key_points: Kinvolved tracks attendance of Harlem schoolchildren and texts parents about tardiness or absences
The user agreement allows the company to change terms at any time, creating future risk
Bankruptcy could lead to valuable tracking data being sold to data brokers
FERPA inadequately protects children because it designates such apps as 'school officials' with 'legitimate interest'
The most vulnerable populations face the greatest surveillance while having the least data protection
money_quotes: What about when they go out of business? I'm thinking the data might be valuable at that point, and their investors might want some money back.
Data brokers would love to get their grubby little hands on such data to add a layer to their profiles of poor black and brown kids.
This is a situation where FERPA, which is the federal child privacy law, is clearly not strong enough.
I'm not singling out Kinvolved for bad intentions; for all I know they mean well and they might even help some kids and families.
It is yet again data concerning the nation's most vulnerable population.
key_insight: Well-intentioned educational technology still creates dangerous surveillance infrastructure over vulnerable communities when data protection laws are inadequate.
